raw_consumer_statement:
In XXXX 2025, while reviewing my American Express business credit card statement, I discovered a charge from HelpMatch LLC related to its healthcare lead-generation platform. That XXXX charge prompted me to review prior statements more carefully, at which point I discovered an additional charge dated XX/XX/year>2025 in the amount of {$1400.00}. 

HelpMatch operates a credit-based system described as usage-based auto-replenishment, in which credits are automatically refilled when balances drop below a threshold. The company states that authorization is considered continuous while enabled and that charges are triggered automatically based on usage rather than a fixed billing cycle. 

No advance billing notice, renewal reminder, or pre-charge notification was sent before the XX/XX/year>2025 charge. I became aware of it only after reviewing my credit card statement in XXXX. 

After discovering the XXXX charge, I immediately contacted HelpMatch and requested : Proof of delivery of any renewal or billing notice Documentation confirming advance notice of the charges Confirmation of authorization for continued automatic replenishment Refund of the unauthorized charges HelpMatch refunded the XXXX charge. However, the company refused to refund the XXXX XXXX, 2025 {$1400.00} charge, despite the same lack of advance notice or renewal reminder. 

In written correspondence, HelpMatch acknowledged that : No advance renewal notices are sent because billing is usage-based rather than calendar-based. 
An invoice is generated only after the charge occurs. 
Changes to billing must be made within their internal dashboard before a refill occurs. 

The company did not provide documentation demonstrating that any advance notice was sent prior to the XXXX charge. No timestamped email headers, transmission logs, or independent proof of delivery were provided. 

Additionally, during monthly meetings with HelpMatch representatives, including a representative named XXXX XXXX, my team communicated that continuation of services was conditioned upon at least one lead converting into a paying client. No leads converted during the engagement period. 

The billing structure effectively allows automatic withdrawals without predictable billing cycles, renewal reminders, or meaningful advance confirmation, and without a clear opportunity to cancel prior to charge. The XXXX charge was refunded once identified, but the XXXX charge remains disputed despite the same lack of notice and authorization clarity. 

I am requesting review of this billing practice and assistance in resolving the disputed {$1400.00} charge.
